course_title: Counselling Approaches and Mental Health Practice
course_description: This course introduces students to working in mental health as a psychotherapist from the perspective of interpersonal psychodynamic theory and one additional evidenced-informed psychotherapy such as CBT utilising focused psychological strategies. The course is designed to deepen students’ understanding of mental health conditions and how to work effectively as a psychotherapist. Students will be expected to be working with clients during the course and will be provided with supervision. Students will apply the basics of assessment and engage in demonstrated application of the specific theories studied.
timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims:
To identify the distinctive features of mental well-being and mental illness.

To understand the nature and components of the human trauma response.

To examine the theoretical foundations of current psychodynamic psychotherapy.

To explore the theoretical foundations of a current evidenced-informed theory such as CBT, Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, or Schema Therapy.

To apply the theoretical principles of two evidenced-informed psychotherapies to mental health practice.

To employ focused psychological strategies to case presentations.

learning_outcomes.description: Have an increased appreciation for the complexity of the human condition and of the neurological and psychological processes involved in human functioning.
Understand the nature trauma conditions and the potential impact that traumatic life events have on the self.
Appreciate the effectiveness of psychotherapy for a wide range of mental health conditions.
Conceptualise and develop comprehensive case formulations based on at least two evidenced-based theories of psychotherapy.
Apply a selected psychotherapy approach to the process of psychotherapy.
Apply a range of focused psychological strategies to case presentations.
Demonstrate critical and reflective reasoning to examine approaches to assessment, treatment planning and psychotherapy.

assessment_details.title: Case Presentation and Discussion
assessment_details.due_date: 1/09/2025 - 22/09/2025 This assessment is presented in class.
assessment_details.task_description: Your task is to present a client case study in either one of two ways. Option 1. Play a video-recoding of a client presentation (available via course resources) and present your analysis of the presenting issue and how you would approach counselling the client using either Psychodynamic Interpersonal Psychotherapy (PIT) or Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) as your theoretical framework. Option 2. Play a recording (audio or video) of a counselling session you have conducted and present a review and analysis of the session. Further details will be provided on blackboard. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Reflective Essay
assessment_details.task_description: Part A: 3000 words In this assignment your task is to write a reflective essay on how you would apply Psychodynamic Interpersonal Therapy (PIT) to a case study provided in class. In reflecting on your application of PIT you should consider the following: A brief description of the case and your case formulation (400 words). An outline of the key principles and processes of PIT (1000 words) An outline of the first four counselling sessions that you would likely conduct and any challenges associated with providing counselling support for the client (800 words) Your essay discussion should include reference to relevant literature to support your argument. Part B: 800 words In addition to a reflection on the application of PIT, you are also required to consider how the case may have been conceptualised differently if ACT was the principal theoretical approach being applied. What might be the strengths and limitations of ACT applied to this case compared to PIT? Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The assignment can be submitted via Turnitin. Instructions will be provided in Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension requests are submitted online via my.UQ. Extension requests received in any other way will not be approved.
Extension guidelines
Extension requests should be submitted prior to the assessment deadline. Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date may not be approved. Late requests must include evidence of the reasons for the late request as per the guidelines in my.UQ . The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of illness or disruption caused by the exceptional circumstances and will typically be no more than the specified maximum extension length for each assessment. A maximum of three assessment requests can be submitted for each assessment, and the third request must include an Assessment Management Plan. Students who have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 4 weeks in a semester are advised to carefully consider whether they are capable of successfully completing their courses and may need to consider applying for removal of course . Students who have a disability or a chronic medical condition (including a mental health condition) are encouraged to meet with a Disability Advisor within Student Services to request a Student Access Plan (SAP) with a clause on extension requests. Students who have an SAP can apply for a 7-day extension.
Outcome of application
Extension requests are generally processed within 2 working days.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.topic: Course Introduction Introduction to the course and the Australian Health System. a. Levels of care b. Types of presentations c. Triage and Referral
Mental Health Competencies a. Mental Health Competencies Framework b. Review of diagnostic approaches c. What works and for whom d. Challenging mental health presentations

Introduction to Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) a. What brings people to therapy? ‘Happiness Traps’, ‘Reality Slaps’ and the ‘Healthy Normality Assumption’ b. The Self in ACT c. Relational Frame Theory as a Network Theory of mental health/disorder d. Evidence-base
Major ACT processes a. Alliance formation b. ACT case formulation c. Skills: Confronting control agenda; psychoeducation through storytelling
Increasing Psychological Flexibility a. Problems in the ‘inner world’: avoidance and fusion b. Skills: acceptance and mindfulness processes c. Applications to common mental health issues
Increasing Behavioural Flexibility a. Skills: Commitments and behaviour change processes b. Applications to common mental health issues
Introduction to Psychodynamic Interpersonal Therapy (PIT) a. An evidence-based approach b. The nature of the self c. Forms of feeling and disruptions of the self
Language, Presence and Forms of Feeling a. Robert Hobson and forms of feeling b. Attending to language c. Attending to state
Essential PIT Skills a. A collaborative stance b. Statements c. Reflecting d. Understanding hypothesis e. Explanatory hypothesis
Play, Symbols and Metaphors a. Explicit and Implicit meaning b. Presentational symbols c. Listening for metaphors and symbols
The Therapeutic Third a. Experience of self and other b. Intimacy and connection
Corrective Experiences a. The change process b. Joining, amplification, representation
Course Review Psychotherapy integration Readiness for practice
